result.content: FINAL_ANSWER: To reconstruct the sequence of sibling-merge operations, perform a post-order traversal (bottom-up) on the final tree. For each node u in the final tree, identify its children v1, v2, ..., vk. These children are the results of merging groups of original siblings from the initial tree. 1. Maintain a mapping (e.g., an array or hash map) that tracks which original node IDs are represented by which node in the current final tree structure. 2. For each node u in the final tree, iterate through its children v1, v2, ..., vk in the order they appear in the final tree's adjacency list. 3. For each child vi, if vi represents a set of original siblings {s1, s2, ..., sm} that were merged, the merge operations are recorded as: - Merge s1 and s2 to get a new node s1,2. - Merge s1,2 and s3 to get s1,2,3, and so on, until sm is incorporated. 4. Because the problem requires a valid sequence, the post-order traversal ensures that all merges occurring within subtrees are completed before the merges at the current level are performed. This respects the dependency that a parent can only be merged after its constituent parts (the children) have been stabilized. 5. Output the sequence of merge operations (u, v) where u and v are the IDs of the nodes being merged at each step. JUSTIFICATION: A post-order traversal ensures that we process the tree from the leaves up to the root. By resolving all merges within the subtrees of the children before merging the children themselves, we satisfy the structural constraints of the tree transformation, ensuring that every merge operation refers to nodes that currently exist in the evolving tree structure.
